text: Writing clean code means writing code that is easy for others to read and understand. A key principle is using proper naming conventions. For example, variable and function names should clearly describe their purpose, like `calculateTotalPrice` instead of just `calc`. Additionally, each function should have a single, specific responsibility. This makes the code simpler to test and modify. When functions are short and focused, the overall system becomes more maintainable. As a result, other developers can quickly grasp the logic and make changes without breaking other parts. In short, clean code is not just for computers—it is for people, too.
